question: Why is assertNever(value: never) needed in a switch?

options.text: It guarantees all union variants are handled — adding a new variant causes a compilation error
It improves switch statement performance
options.explanation: assertNever doesn't affect performance — it's a compile-time completeness checking tool

options.text: It automatically handles unknown variants without errors
options.explanation: On the contrary: assertNever throws a runtime error and creates a compilation error if not all variants are handled

options.text: It allows using switch without a default branch
options.explanation: assertNever is specifically used IN the default branch to verify handling completeness

question: What is an algebraic data type (ADT) in the TypeScript context?

options.text: A tagged union with a discriminant field and constructor functions
Any type defined via a type alias
options.explanation: Not every type alias is an ADT — ADT implies a sum type (tagged union) structure with discriminants

options.text: A class with abstract methods
options.explanation: ADTs in TypeScript are implemented via tagged unions, not through classes and inheritance

options.text: A type that supports arithmetic operations
options.explanation: 'Algebraic' refers to type algebra (sum and product), not arithmetic

question: How does Extract<AppEvent, { type: 'USER_LOGIN' }> extract the needed variant from a union?

options.text: It keeps only those union members that are assignable to the specified shape
It creates a new type by merging AppEvent with { type: 'USER_LOGIN' }
options.explanation: Extract filters, not merges — it removes incompatible variants

options.text: It removes the type field from the resulting type
options.explanation: Extract doesn't remove fields — it returns the full variant type including all its properties

options.text: It checks types only at runtime
options.explanation: Extract works entirely at the type level, producing no runtime code

question: What requirements must a discriminant in a discriminated union satisfy?

options.text: Be a literal type (string or numeric literal)
Be present in all union variants
Have a unique value in each variant
Be exclusively a string type
options.explanation: A discriminant can be a numeric literal, boolean, or even an enum value — not only a string

options.text: Be declared with the readonly modifier
options.explanation: readonly is recommended as a best practice but is not a mandatory requirement for the discriminant to work

question: How is Option<T> better than null/undefined for representing an absent value?

options.text: Forces explicit handling of both cases (value present / value absent)
Enables building operation chains via map/flatMap
Runs faster than null checks
options.explanation: Option creates an additional wrapper object, which is slower than direct null checks. The advantage is type safety, not performance

options.text: Automatically throws an error when accessing an absent value
options.explanation: Option does NOT throw errors — it forces you to explicitly handle the none case, instead of getting a runtime null reference error

options.text: Makes the possibility of absence an explicit part of the type signature
